title: Acute Limb Ischemia
chief_complaint: My right leg suddenly became cold, pale, and painful 4 hours ago. I can barely move my toes and I can't feel my foot. I have atrial fibrillation and I'm not on blood thinners.

physical_exam.Right_Leg: Classic 6 Ps: Pain (severe, constant), Pallor (porcelain white), Pulselessness (absent femoral, popliteal, DP, PT pulses), Paresthesia (decreased sensation in foot - ominous sign), Paralysis (unable to move toes - VERY ominous), Poikilothermia (cold).
physical_exam.Cardiac: Irregularly irregular rhythm (atrial fibrillation). No murmurs.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Right foot: Sensation severely decreased. Motor: Toe flexion/extension 1/5 (late finding - indicates threatened limb).

hidden_data.ct_angiogram: Abrupt cutoff of right common femoral artery with no distal flow. Left iliofemoral system patent. No significant atherosclerosis (embolic occlusion - classic for AFib).
hidden_data.ecg: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response 110 bpm. No ST changes.
hidden_data.echo: Left atrial enlargement. Spontaneous echo contrast in left atrial appendage. No thrombus visualized but high suspicion.
hidden_data.labs: WBC 12,500, Hb 13.5, CK 450 (early elevation - muscle ischemia), Lactate 3.5, K+ 4.8, Creatinine 1.2

correct_diagnosis: Acute Limb Ischemia (Rutherford Class IIb - Immediately Threatened) from Cardioembolic Occlusion of Right Common Femoral Artery secondary to Atrial Fibrillation
key_learning_points: Acute limb ischemia = sudden decrease in limb perfusion. Causes: Embolism (80% - cardiac source: AFib, MI, endocarditis, prosthetic valve), Thrombosis (atherosclerotic plaque rupture, graft occlusion), Trauma. Emboli typically lodge at bifurcations (common femoral, popliteal, brachial)

Rutherford classification: I (Viable): No sensory loss, no motor weakness, Doppler signals present. IIa (Marginally threatened): Minimal sensory loss, no motor weakness, arterial Doppler inaudible, venous audible. IIb (Immediately threatened): Sensory loss more than toes, motor weakness, arterial/venous Doppler inaudible. III (Irreversible): Profound sensory loss, paralysis, rigor mortis, no Doppler signals - AMPUTATION required

Classic 6 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia. Progression: Paresthesia then Paralysis indicate nerve then muscle death. Irreversible damage: Nerve 4-6h, Muscle 6-8h

Immediate treatment: 1) Heparin bolus 80 U/kg IV then 18 U/kg/hr (prevent proximal and distal propagation of thrombus). 2) Pain control, IVF, protect limb (padding, avoid pressure). 3) Vascular surgery consult EMERGENTLY. 4) Definitive: Catheter-directed thrombolysis (tPA) for viable/marginally threatened. Surgical embolectomy (Fogarty catheter) for threatened/embolic. Bypass for thrombotic occlusion. Fasciotomy if delayed >4-6h (compartment syndrome)

Reperfusion injury: Release of potassium, lactate, myoglobin from ischemic muscle → hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, AKI, cardiac arrhythmias. Monitor closely. Amputation if irreversible (Rutherford III). Post-op: Anticoagulation (warfarin/DOAC for AFib), treat underlying cause
